artifact.text:
Was ist das eigentlich — what, in fact, is a people?

Two roots sit under the English word and the room kept reaching past both of them. There is *populus*, the plural Roman political body, the *senatus populusque* whose verb may be singular but whose noun stays plural even after the legionnaires have marched away. And there is *Volk*, the romantic substance of the nineteenth century — organic, pre-political, defined by descent. When Senator Vance speaks of a particular people rooted in generations of residence, he has reached for the second. When Secretary Clinton answers him with the creed, she reaches for the first — but as singularity, as a unitary essence to be defended, not as the plural of citizens organized by law and acting in concert. The quarrel over which kind of unity "the American people" is leaves the singular grammar undisturbed. The townships, the conventions, the federalist plurality of plural acting bodies that the founders' actual practice supplied — these have dropped out of the argument before it begins.

A sentence is the hinge. President Pavel said: *experience shows populists moderate once they bear governing responsibility*. I have heard this sentence before. Not in those words; in that shape. The new phenomenon will be domesticated by the apparatus that handles all phenomena. Hindenburg's circle believed it. Much of the Centre Party believed it. Much of the German liberal press believed it through the spring of 1933. They were not stupid; they were thinking with categories that had worked, until they didn't. The discipline that comes from having watched those categories fail is to ask, when offered such a sentence: how do we know this phenomenon is the kind that responds to those mechanisms? Sometimes — and this is the harder admission — we do not know. Categorical novelty has to be earned by the analysis, not declared by reflex; not every right-populism is a totalitarian formation in nascent form. But *will moderate* is the sentence I have learned to mistrust on principle, because it taught me what mistrust on principle is for.

Decompose his other phrase: *listening to citizens' frustration and translating it into policy is what politics is for*. Frustration is a passion, and a diffuse one — a passion of the guts addressed to no particular speech of any particular citizen, available to whoever channels it loudest. Its opposite is *doxa*: the worked, considered showing-forth of how-it-seems-to-me, formed where citizens speak to one another and let their views be tested in the company of others. *Doxa* requires a space of appearance — an *Erscheinungsraum*, which springs up where plural humans become visible to one another in speech and deed. Frustration requires only a polling instrument. When the political class accepts frustration as its input, it has already conceded the ground on which politics in the proper sense would be conducted at all.

MEP Weber's firewall and Pavel's accommodation share the same conceptual error. The three pros and the three cons are tests applied to parties taken as given; the party form itself — the institutional carrier of "the people's voice" between elections — is what the mass-media moment, and now the platforms, have hollowed into a polling-aggregate. Firewall, accommodation, governing-responsibility: three managers of the same residue.

Representative Ocasio-Cortez does something the others do not, and the difference is worth noticing. She tells a scene: a closed-door meeting in which party leadership derided as too extreme the things particular working people in her district were in fact calling for. Particular speakers, particular demands, a particular dismissal. That is not yet a singular subject. And when she names extreme inequality and monopolies grown to rival nation-states, she names what *Origins* called superfluity — the experience of being made economically unneeded, the prepolitical condition that destroys plurality and on which mass movements grow. Then her sentence generalizes — *the* working class, *the* base, *the* demands — and the unitary noun reasserts itself in her speech as well. This is not her failure of attention. It is the available political grammar. Whoever wants to speak now must speak through that noun or be unintelligible.

So which populist moment is the apparatus equipped to name? The prior collapse. The demos in the strong sense — plural distinct equals appearing to one another in speech and deed — is what has already gone missing when the question of populism arises in the form the panel posed. Firewall and accommodation quarrel over the residue; governing-responsibility prophesies its taming; the noun that none of them interrogates is what each of them takes for granted: that there is a single voice somewhere, sullen or enraged or simply tired, waiting to be properly channeled.

The recovery, where it comes, comes plural and small and fragile — the townships when they were what they were, the unions when they were associations of speakers and not providers of services, the workers' councils in Budapest for the eleven days they lasted in 1956. The form has not stabilized into a regime in any case I know. I am not handing anyone a procedure. "The people" in the singular is not the data of politics. It is the residue of the politics one has already lost. Whoever proposes to manage that residue more cleverly has not yet noticed what is missing from the table.
artifact.selected_form: Short essay opening on a buried question, anchored in the etymological doublet and the single sentence, leaving the question reformulated at the close.
artifact.stance: Cooler than indignation, drier than alarm — the dryness of someone who has heard a sentence before and is naming the shape it has, not raising her voice about the danger.
